item.title: How Swap Markets Are Struggling to Gauge Rates as Iran War Fuels Volatility
item.description: The Iran war has clouded the outlook for inflation and economic growth across the globe and, in turn, where interest rates might be heading.

item.title: Sudan Gold Exports to UAE Slump After Diplomatic Ties Severed
item.description: Sudanese gold exports to the United Arab Emirates plunged last year, after the military-backed government ended relations with the Gulf state.

item.title: Onetime Elon Musk Whisperer Shares the ‘Algorithm’
item.description: When Jon McNeill left Tesla in 2018, he took with him a reputation as a growth driver and fix-it man. If you can manage Musk’s special brand of chaos, the thinking goes, you can run anything.

item.title: ECB Encourages Banks to Test Lending Offers as Reserves Shrink
item.description: Banks should prepare to tap regular loan offers by the European Central Bank as quantitative tightening proceeds and liquidity sloshing around the financial system shrinks, according to a blog post published Thursday.

item.title: Spring Break Bills Skyrocket for Wealthy Americans Vacationing at the Beach
item.description: The Naples Beach Club, a Four Seasons resort on the Gulf Coast of Florida, opened in November as a lodging industry rarity: a new luxury hotel built directly on a US beach.

item.title: Neoen to Build France’s Largest Battery Amid Strained Power Grid
item.description: Renewable energy developer Neoen will build France’s largest battery, helping to store rising solar and wind generation and ease strain on the country’s grid.

item.title: ECB’s Panetta Says Even If Iran War Ends Damage Has Been Done
item.description: Damage caused by the US’s war against Iran will continue to have a negative impact on the global economy even if hostilities end soon, according to European Central Bank Governing Council member Fabio Panetta.

item.title: Swiss Franc Set to Reclaim Haven Role, Options Trading Suggests
item.description: The Swiss franc hasn’t been playing its traditional haven role of late, even as the war in Iran keeps geopolitics fraught. Options trading suggests the anomaly won’t last.

item.title: Volvo Says Iran War Hit US Demand, Signaling Wider Auto Pain
item.description: Volvo Car AB said the war in Iran hurt US demand in the first quarter, offering one of the clearest signs yet that the conflict is starting to dent sales as higher fuel prices curb spending.

item.title: ECB’s Cipollone Sees ‘Good Momentum’ for Digital Euro Project
item.description: European Central Bank Executive Board member Piero Cipollone sees “good momentum” for a digital euro, with a possible launch in July 2029.

item.title: First LNG Ship to Attempt Hormuz Exit Isn’t Carrying a Cargo
item.description: A liquefied natural gas tanker has entered the Strait of Hormuz, and if it successfully navigates the waterway would become the first such vessel to pass through the strait since the start of the war.

item.title: ‘Market’s Latest Shiny Objects’: Optical Stocks Get AI Momentum
item.description: Over the past few years, the build-out of artificial intelligence infrastructure has transformed stocks in a variety of industries, from chips to utilities, into unexpected high-fliers. Optical component makers are investors’ latest darlings.

item.title: Chinese Copper Miners Join $1.2 Billion African Rail Revamp
item.description: Chinese mining, shipping and logistics companies are joining a $1.24 billion project to revamp a railway linking Zambia’s copper region to a port on the Indian ocean.

item.title: As Stocks Fall, Easing Volatility in Korea Signals Some Hope
item.description: While stocks slumped across markets in the wake of US President Donald Trump’s address, a measure in South Korea gave some traders a degree of comfort.

item.title: Foreign Selling of Japan Stocks Hits 18-Month High on Iran Risk
item.description: Foreigners offloaded the most Japanese shares since September 2024 last week in another sign of growing fears about the impact of the Iran war on the Asian economy.

item.title: ECB’s Simkus Says Too Early to Call Outcome of April’s Meeting
item.description: It’s premature to say what the European Central Bank should do at next month’s interest-rate meeting as the situation around the Iran war is moving by the day, according to Governing Council member Gediminas Simkus.

item.title: European Diesel Futures Top $200 as Iran War Hits Supply
item.description: Europe’s diesel futures rose to the highest level since 2022, as the Iran war hits supply of the fuel that powers the global economy.

item.title: JPMorgan Asset Buys Treasuries, Gilts Dented by Inflation Risks
item.description: JPMorgan Asset Management is buying government bonds including those from the US and the UK, betting inflation concerns have left some parts of the market oversold.

item.title: This Is How to Tell if Writing Was Made by AI
item.description: The growing slopification of the written word.

item.title: Odd Lots: How to Tell if Writing Was Made by AI (Podcast)
item.description: Odd Lots: How to Tell if Writing Was Made by AI (Podcast)【原文：Odd Lots: How to Tell if Writing Was Made by AI (Podcast)】

item.title: Thai Songkran Holiday Spending Set for Biggest Drop Since 2022
item.description: Thailand’s Songkran festival — the country’s biggest annual celebration of water fights, homecomings and holiday spending — will be more subdued this year as rising costs weigh on consumers.

item.title: A 50% Crash in Key Hungarian Stock Signals Trouble for Orban
item.description: For years, 4iG Nyrt has been a crown jewel of Viktor Orban’s economy, a sprawling telecoms and defense powerhouse with a soaring stock price. But now the shares are plunging and sending perhaps the clearest signal yet that investors are preparing for the end of the Hungarian prime minister’s 16-year reign.

item.title: Air France-KLM Submits First Bid for Stake in Portugal’s TAP
item.description: One of Europe’s biggest airline groups kicked off the race for TAP SA, with Air France-KLM submitting the first known bid for a stake in the Portuguese flag carrier.

item.title: Segantii Insider Trading Case Began on Bank of America Red Flags
item.description: One of Hong Kong’s most high-profile insider trading cases began after compliance staff at Bank of America Corp., the firm arranging a block trade, flagged concerns about handling price-sensitive information that wasn’t public.

item.title: China Tells Private Refiners to Keep Up Fuel Output at All Costs
item.description: Chinese officials have told private refiners to keep fuel production at 2025 levels — even if they have to incur economic losses as a month-long war in the Middle East upends the global crude oil trade.

item.title: Swiss Inflation Jumps to Fastest in a Year on Iran War Impact
item.description: Switzerland’s inflation rate jumped in March to the quickest pace in a year as the energy supply crunch caused by the war in the Middle East stoked the cost of heating oil.

item.title: Australia Broadens Fuel Tax Cut in Attempt to Shield Consumers
item.description: Australia will further cut fuel taxes to cushion households from rising prices as the Iran war upends global supply chains.

item.title: FTSE 100 Futures, Pound Fall on War Escalation Worries
Hong Kong’s Small Builder Debt Woes Mount Despite Property Rally
item.description: Some of Hong Kong’s small property developers are grappling with renewed liquidity strains despite signs of recovery in the local real estate market, putting them on shakier ground than larger players in the sector.

item.title: Asian Nations Jockey For Leverage to Re-Open Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Countries across Asia are jockeying for leverage and adopting splintering approaches as they respond to US President Donald Trump’s call to open the Strait of Hormuz by themselves, amid mounting economic disruptions.
